planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic, fertile, loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; white jaboticaba is frost-sensitive (damaged below 30°F) and requires a frost-free climate. Like all jaboticabas, it is slow-growing but long-lived, producing fruit on the trunk and main branches in a spectacular cauliflorous display.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic, fertile, loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; white jaboticaba (also called yellow jaboticaba) is a Brazilian species of jaboticaba producing smaller fruits than the common jaboticaba (Plinia cauliflora), with the distinctive yellow-green to white skin of the ripe grape-like fruits distinguishing it from the deep purple-black common jaboticaba. Like all jaboticabas, the fruits grow caulifloresly — directly on the trunk and main branches of the tree rather than on the branch tips — creating the remarkable, otherworldly spectacle of hundreds of fruits clustering directly on the bark. The flavor of white jaboticaba is sweeter and less astringent than the more common purple types, with a delicate, pleasant muscat-like sweetness. Used for fresh eating, jams, and fruit wines. Rare and highly prized even in Brazil, much rarer outside its native range than the common jaboticaba. A prized collector's fruit for zone 9–11 gardens where unusual tropical fruits are cultivated.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average, loamy or sandy soil in full sun; ketembilla is frost-sensitive but tolerates brief dips to 28°F when established. Dioecious — both male and female plants are needed for fruit production; plant at least one of each.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average, loamy or sandy soil in full sun; ketembilla (Ceylon gooseberry) is a thorny, multi-stemmed shrub from Sri Lanka producing small (1/2 to 1 inch diameter), round, reddish-purple to deep purple fruits with a tart, acidic, gooseberry-like flavor that makes them outstanding for jams, jellies, preserves, and syrups — not particularly pleasant raw due to their sharp acidity, but among the best tropical fruits for processing. The plant is heavily thorned and makes an impenetrable hedge — widely used in Sri Lanka and tropical gardens for security hedging. Requires both male and female plants for fruit (dioecious). Grows quickly in warm, humid, well-drained conditions. The tart fruits are exceptionally high in vitamin C. In Florida it is occasionally grown as a specialty fruit and hedge plant. Underutilized as a jam fruit in the US — the intense, tart purple jam from ketembilla rivals the finest domestic berry jams.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to average, sandy or loamy, lean to average soil in full sun; karanda is drought-tolerant and salt-tolerant once established, growing naturally in dry scrub forests and coastal areas of India and Southeast Asia. More cold-tolerant than natal plum, surviving brief dips to 24°F.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to average, sandy or loamy, lean to average soil in full sun; karanda is a thorny, multi-stemmed shrub or small tree from India, Sri Lanka, and Southeast Asia — widely used throughout South Asia as an impenetrable security hedge and as a productive fruit shrub. The small, oval fruits (1/2 to 1 inch) ripen from green through white to pink and finally red-purple or black, with the white stage being particularly popular for pickling (used extensively in Indian pickles, chutneys, and preserves) while the ripe red-black fruits are eaten fresh or used for jams. The fruits have a mild, slightly tart flavor when ripe. Extremely drought and heat tolerant — one of the tougher fruiting shrubs for hot, dry gardens in zones 8–11. More cold-tolerant than natal plum (Carissa macrocarpa), making it useful in the warmer parts of zone 8 where natal plum would be damaged. Exudes a milky latex sap when cut — wear gloves when pruning. Forms an excellent thorny security hedge.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky, sandy or clay soil in full sun; ring muhly grows naturally in dry, rocky, desert grassland and piñon-juniper woodland soils of the Southwest at 3,500–7,000 feet elevation. Extremely drought tolerant once established, requiring essentially no supplemental water in appropriate climates.

directions: Direct sow in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky, sandy or clay soil in full sun; ring muhly is a low, tufted, fine-leaved native bunchgrass of dry southwestern desert grassland and piñon-juniper woodland, producing wiry, thread-like leaves and delicate, airy seed panicles. Named for the 'fairy ring' pattern that develops as old clumps die out in the center while continuing to grow at the outer rim — over many years, a single plant produces an ever-expanding ring of grass that leaves a bare center, similar to the fairy rings of mushrooms. The rings can reach 2–4 feet in diameter in old, undisturbed specimens. The fine, wiry texture and extremely low water needs make ring muhly an excellent ornamental groundcover grass for hot, rocky, dry southwestern garden settings. An excellent low-maintenance native grass for piñon-juniper landscaping, xeriscape rock gardens, and restoration plantings in the Colorado Plateau, New Mexico, and Arizona mountain foothills.

planting.note: Direct sow after soil warms in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, sandy or loamy, lean soil in full sun; mesa dropseed grows naturally on dry, open desert mesas, sandy plains, and desert grassland of the Chihuahuan and Sonoran deserts and Great Basin transition zones. Extremely heat and drought tolerant — requires no supplemental irrigation in appropriate climates.

directions: Direct sow after soil warms in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, sandy or loamy, lean soil in full sun; mesa dropseed is a medium-sized warm-season bunchgrass of desert mesas and sandy desert grasslands from the Texas Panhandle and New Mexico through Arizona and into the Great Basin — one of the characteristic dropseed grasses (Sporobolus) of the hot, dry southwestern lowlands. The airy, open, finely branched panicles are ornamental and distinctive. 'Dropseed' refers to the characteristic of the genus where the tiny, shiny seeds fall (drop) as soon as they are ripe, spreading quickly around the parent plant. An important forage grass for pronghorn, desert mule deer, and livestock on desert ranges. One of the better grasses for very hot, dry, sandy desert-grassland restoration planting mixes across the Southwest. Related to prairie dropseed (S. heterolepis, of the northern prairies) but adapted to much hotter, drier conditions. Fast-germinating and establishing from seed in appropriate warm, dry conditions.
